next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

By the second reading, the pattern was too clean to dismiss. The base layer at the bottom of the Mark had strengthened again. Hermione wrote the measurement in the ledger, checked last week's entry, then the week before. The rise was steady. Every long examination moved it.

That correlation stayed out of the ledger.

"I'm going to push lower," she said.

Draco kept his eyes on the book propped beside his hand. "Go on."

Magic slid under her fingers, through the degraded lattice of Voldemort's binding, past the defensive dark matter that still fed on his core, down to the buried layer that did not belong with the rest. The contact gave way. The room went wrong.

There was the library, the fire, the table, her own hand on his wrist.

There was also the room from his side of it: the crease in the page under his thumb, the weight of his arm on the padding, the exact place where her touch rested. It was not just physical sensation. Her nearness, registered from inside him with humiliating clarity. The ink on her fingers. The wool at her wrist. The fact that his attention had been on her for longer than the book in front of him.

Under that sat something worse. Shame, old and ground deep, wrapped around the black thing on his arm. Then came the answering ease when her palm held it down, when the brand quieted and his body, traitorous and simple, took the relief as hers.

Hermione tore her hand away.

The doubled view collapsed. Her fingertips buzzed. Across the table Draco had already set the book down.

"What happened."

"I caught a spillover." Even now her voice...
open_hooks_to_carry: Post-drawing-room gentleness rendered in small domestic moments: shared meals, books left on chairs, tea made without asking
Draco cataloguing Hermione's expressions — her laugh, her sleeping in the library — registering that he is doing this
Theo Nott's visit: Draco with a friend, drier and funnier, the dynamic revealing who he is when not armored
prev_tail_excerpt:
...n't sure whether she meant don't say that or don't make me answer it. "I'm not telling you you should've died."

"No. You're telling me you can explain why I lived." His mouth worked once. "You keep saying you testified for me as if it closes the account."

That landed because it was true. She did keep reaching for the trial, for evidence, for the clean legal version of the night. None of that had altered the room.

"I don't know how to do this," she said. Not the conversation. The house. The work. The library upstairs still warm from tea. "I don't know how to stay here and know this room exists. I don't know how to look at you and not see the wall."

That was the point at which his legs failed him. No gesture in it, no strategy. He slid down the paneling until he was sitting on the marble with his forearms braced over his knees, head bowed like the body had simply run out of instructions.

No apology followed. None would have been enough. The room had already taken everything it could take.

She turned and walked out.

Crossing from marble to flagstone felt like stepping out of a cellar and into ordinary weather. The corridor stayed fixed around her: portraits, stone, cracked flagstone, guest wing ahead. Nothing shifted. The house had no need. It had already shown her what it wanted shown.

At the crack in the floor, the resonance caught again, altered beyond recognition. For weeks it had lived at the edge of her attention as warmth. Now it came through as cold stone, held breath, a grief dense enough to feel like weight. He was still in the drawing room. She kept walking.
secondary_character_beats: Theo Nott as a full person: post-war Slytherin navigating suspicion, pragmatic, bitter in flashes, genuinely loyal. He sees the situation with Hermione immediately and says almost nothing about it except one honest line to Draco alone
state_deltas_required: The aftermath of trauma confrontation — both characters navigating a new register of honesty while trying to continue the work
Tenderness enters without permission. Domesticity becomes habitual. A third register emerges between them: not professional, not traumatic, but quiet and almost fond
